speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about your brother, Moshe.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, Moshe, his wife. He had two children-- a girl that was that time, about seven, and an boy. Her name was Chani. And the boy's name was Shloime. He was about five or four and a half, five years old. He had nowhere-- nowhere to go. He stayed in-- in his house. This happened two weeks-- I-- I would say, August the 2nd. This must have been in July, the middle of July, that my two brothers' wives and children were away from Torun. But we still stay-- we still stayed in Torun. On the night of the-- of the Tisha B'av night of the 2 and 3, there was a labor unit. A Jewish labor unit was assigned, but they-- those were not from Torun. Those were Jewish boys from deep in Hungary. They were-- they were assigned to this-- to this Hungarian brigade that was there. I remember I went to the s-- to the synagogue. It's u-- it's customary to on a-- on a-- on a fast day, especially on Tisha B'av, fast day, where you fast. But you talk about the destruction of the-- of the [NON-ENGLISH], of the holy temple that took place. We are now in 1941. It took place, that time, about 1,920 years ago-- that you say [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH]. I was sitting in the- in the-- in the-- synagogue. And somehow, I-- I-- my mood wasn't-- wasn't too-- too good. And I went out. And across from the synagogue was a house where there was a-- a couple that the father died not-- not too long before and also the mother. And there were three sisters and-- and-- and one older sister that was married that lived in this house, and two younger sisters and two-- and two boys. And in that house, they hou-- housed a Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], a Jewish group. And I heard them play and sing and play on the harmonica. So this was a-- a very-- you know, for young people to go together. Because those were young, young people. And then the older sister was like the matriarch of the house. And it was always a house that was-- was young people would congregate. And it was always fun to be there. So I went over there. And-- and I see this group. One was playing accordion. Another one had-- had mandolin. And they were playing-- and those were Jewish boys. And they were playing these-- these Hungarian-- Hungarian folk songs. And it kind of struck me. Because I said, those guys are so assimilated. They didn't know Yiddish. Those-- those boys came from-- really, from deeper inside there. They don't even know what kind of day it is today. [INAUDIBLE] they are playing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed there. I stayed there, maybe, for a half an hour or so. And-- and then I decided this is not for me. And I went over. I went to my-- to my house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went and got into my bedroom. I [INAUDIBLE] my brother Tsvi and I-- Tsvi came in later. I don't remember where he was. He came in later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember telling him, did you hear these guys over there? They're-- they're singing. And they're having fun and playing. And today is Tisha B'av.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's not a day-- day of singing. And it must have been around 11:00, 11-- maybe 12 o'clock. And we both went-- went to sleep. About-- we just fell asleep, about an hour or so, an hour or an hour and a half later. All of a sudden, I hear big banging in the floor, in the-- in the door, and in the windows. Open up. Open up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother jumped out of bed. And he said, boy-- and we thought that those are those Jewish boys banging at the door to open up. And just to tell you that this didn't even-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] boy, am I going to get-- give them the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: It must've been around 1:30, 2 o'clock at night. When we opened up the-- up the door here were gendarmes, Hungarian gendarmes with the-- those were-- those were a group of people, the most brutal group of people, that they-- they-- kept [INAUDIBLE]. They were known-- known. And they gave us the order. We could smell-- we could smell the whiskey from them. They gave them a lot of whiskey to-- to-- you know, to encourage them. And they ordered us-- you have a half an hour's time. You get dressed. And you get-- get the hell out of here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What? Where? When? Who? Who said it? They had the-- the-- the-- rifles, rifle butts.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were banging on our-- in a very [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] meshuggeneh voice-- voices. The way they were. Anyway, anyway, this is how it started. We-- we turned the lights on. And we started getting dressed. And they said to us that we are taking you. [INAUDIBLE] you have to get dressed. You can take-- you can take some clothing along. We're going to take you to a labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: A labor camp? But they took-- took my father, my mother. My mother was 60-some years old. My father was 67. My [INAUDIBLE]-- my mother was a year or two younger.

speaker: interviewee
text: And already, at-- at-- they started going around in the room, from room to room, started opening up drawers. On my night table, there was-- I put on my wristwatch on the night table. He grabbed the one-- the one's grabbed the wristwatch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I saw him put it in the pocket. And I said-- and I started to [INAUDIBLE] what are you? Why are you taking my wristwatch?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got hit with a-- with a-- with a-- clink-- with a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rifle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rifle. Got hit with rifle. So I-- I-- I stopped. I stopped protesting. I saw what-- and I-- and I never understand why my mother told me. You know, I wanted to put on the everyday clothing that I was wearing. She said, put on the newer clothing. It always stuck into-- into me, why? Why she did she say that? And-- But I remember. It's stuck in my memory. Put on be-- the newer, the better clothing. We put on. And then the-- the synagogue was just, like, across the street here. That's how close it was. And they took us out. Yeah, I have to say, we had a maid, a Russian maid in the house. They took us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And brother sent to the synagogue. When we got into the synagogue, I saw already, there is a whole bunch of people in there. Everybody started, well, what's happening here?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- what-- you know, everybody was asking. Nobody had the answers. And they didn't-- didn't know. In front already, where the [NON-ENGLISH], on the front, there was-- we saw the local notar the-- from the community. And my father had dealing-- dealings with him and knew him well. And there were two in civilian clothing that we never saw them before. Those were what the Hungarians called [NON-ENGLISH], detectives or [INAUDIBLE]. They were there. And they had a list. They had a list. And gendarmes and police were bringing out, bringing in more and more families into the synagogue from the entire-- entire community. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was such a shock that we were-- brains stopped-- stopped thinking. We didn't-- we didn't know. We didn't know what to do, what to think. Fortunately for us, they brought in my brother. That was Moshe, his wife, and the two children, they brought in. They brought up my-- my uncles and other family. And we knew each other in a small community. You had 135 families. You knew--- you knew every family, even. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this notar, the local notar-- he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- this was really a miracle. He said-- he looked at the list. And he said that Abraham Jakuvobics is not on the list, because he is 75 years of age. I told you that this was wrongly listed or something. Because he wasn't. And it was-- his-- his-- [INAUDIBLE] under his-- he had the authority. And he said to my mother and my father, you go back to your house. And soldiers-- he ordered soldiers to escort them back. They go back to their house. And-- and it turned out-- it turned out that out of the 135 families, they had 18, 17 or 18 people that were listed above 75 years of age, that those people were spared. They were left-- left-- left home. As soon as we-- we saw our parents, are-- are out, the anxiety was lessened. At least-- at least, for us, I mean, my brother Tsvi-- I was 21. And-- and-- and he was 3-- 32, 33. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we didn't-- when they-- they-- they brought in everybody. But we didn't see our brother, Eliezer, the oldest brother. We didn't see him then, in there. And we didn't know what happened. We found out five months later, that he was alone in his house. Because his wife and-- and the children were in Debrecen. When they banged at his door, at the front door, there was an exit in the [INAUDIBLE]. And he somehow had a premonition. He-- he-- because this happened on Sunday night. And Friday, I remember in-- in during the day, he was in our house. And he said, I don't like what's going on. He used to be-- when the Czechs were still there, he used to be a councilman. He was elected from the Zionist movement as a councilman in city council. So he-- he- would get into the city. And he knew-- he knew the people. He said, I knew the people. And I see there is something going on secretly that I wasn't invited. I wasn't told.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they started banging at his door, he managed to put some clothing on and got out in the back. And he escaped. And he escaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know that then. We found out later. It took a few days. He managed to escape and get reunited with his wife in Debrecen. And so were left-- we were left, the three brothers, Moshe, Tsvi, and I, Moshe and his wife and the two children. Tsvi's wife was with her parents. We didn't know what's happening to her. We also just found out five months later when we managed to come back. And there was-- we-- when I think back and in this pandemonium, the-- the-- the-- the cries of the children in this synagogue, huge hall and-- and- and-- and-- and-- and the great pandemonium, I still think I-- I could have escaped there. I could have run out. And I tried to talk to my brother Tsvi, because we were closer. I said, I think we can-- we can get out of here. It's-- it's a pandemonium. They can't-- c-- He says, no, we can't do that. We are not going to leave Moshe with the two children. Like, we can do help them something. He goes, no, we should-- we shouldn't do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we waited till dawn in the synagogue. And all of a sudden, we saw buses. Buses came in. And they started loading the people up. Where are we going? Where? Nobody had an answer. You're going to work. All? The babies? The old? The women? The children? So they put us on the buses. And we made sure-- we made sure that the three of us kept together, you know, that we don't get separated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] early in the morning, I don't know with these buses, how many people got in there, 70, 80, you know, with children in the-- in the bus. And they started us in the direction to the city of Khust. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we passed-- when we passed villages that we knew, you know, the next village was Ober Bistra. The next village was Majdan. The next one was [INAUDIBLE]. The next one was Walawa. Walawa was the-- was the county seat. Walawa was already the largest-- larger community. Walawa had maybe 5,000, 6,000 population. And there was maybe 1,200 Jewish people lived there. And they had the courthouse there. A little hospital was already there. They started deporting the people from closest to the border first. So we were one of the first, because we were at the last village to the border. So when-- and we saw the people when we passed these.

speaker: interviewer
text: We saw people going to synagogue. You know, it's Tisha B'av. And they couldn't-- and we saw the bewilderment on their faces. What's going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: We see all these people from Torun. We knew-- we knew most of the people, even in the next village, 3, 4 kilometers away. So it took-- it took I think-- normally, it takes about 3, 4 hours. At that time, the roads were not paved like they are now. 80 kilometers, you could probably make it in an hour, hour and a half. But it took a couple hours, two, three hours. We got into Khust. And there they brought us into a place where it was a-- a huge lumber yard. But it was fenced off with high fences and all around. So they brought us in there into the open field. And they started unload-- unloading us. Still, still, there were gendarmes already there.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were military there. Still, we didn't get an answer. What? Where are we going? What are you doing? The-- the-- the-- the-- the plain answer was we're going to go to work. Work? With-- with children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we got unloaded. And of course, during the day, we took along some-- some food that we had with us for that day. And it was a nice sunny day, August the 3. And by coincidence-- I-- I just looked that up not too long ago that the-- the expulsion of the Jews in 19-- in 1492 from Spain, the edict that Isabella ordered was given out in the month of March. And they g-- they got four months to either convert or get out. The last Jews left on Tisha B'av, which was August the 7-- the 2, 194-- 1492. Columbus sailed to the United States a day later on-- after Tisha B'av on-- on August the 3, 1492. It's just a coincidence that all of these tragedies coincided at-- at the same day. Well, we stayed there during the day. And I took a walk around-- around the perimeters of the huge lumber yard. And I noticed that there were a few-- a few of the-- of the lumbers, you know, the fence that were loose. And with a good push, you can probably push it out and get out. And I-- and I still remember talking to my brother Tsvi and saying, let's-- let's try to get out of here. And his-- his answer was the same. We're not going to leave Moshe and the children. We can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the-- the fact is, where would we have gone? But we didn't know. We thought that in Khust, there are still some. Anyway, we stayed overnight.

speaker: interviewee
text: We made our beds. Nobody slept. It was a-- a-- a-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the sky-- the sky was clear. And-- and-- and-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the-- the-- the stars were out. And we were out on that field that night. Of course, we didn't sleep. And there were so many babies, children, how the parents tried to make comfortable for these children. They were not speaking about facilities for-- for-- human facilities that was needed for them and the children. It-- it-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the brain was just numb. We survived that night. And a day later, the next day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they loaded us up on trucks, military trucks from there, no buses, military trucks. And this was-- this was, again, the great screams and cries. Because on the trucks, you could only put up so many, 40, 50 people. And you-- everybody wanted to be together, a family together. They had children. They had brother-in-laws, you know, that the families were not torn. We were lucky to keep together, the three brothers. The three of us, we got into-- into that-- that-- and then we started going. And-- and we didn't know where we are going. We didn't know the direction. Up to the city of Khust, we knew. We knew because this was a difference. It turned out that they were sending us to another side of the Carpathian, by the border, called Yasinia. And I'll never forget. There was a-- a man by the name of [? Yasil ?] Davidovich. He was a heavyset man and a very, very wise man. And he was one on the-- on that bus. Not bus, on that-- on that truck. I'll continue with that.
